summary: Spolsky criticizes Gartner's recommendation that Microsoft completely rewrite IIS to address security vulnerabilities. He argues that Gartner falls into the common trap of believing new code is inherently less buggy than old code. He points out that IIS has been battle-tested for six years across millions of servers, and a rewrite would simply introduce a fresh set of unknown bugs. He dismisses the recommendation as reflecting the mindset of immature developers who lack real software experience.
key_points: Completely rewriting software does not make it less buggy — it simply trades known bugs for unknown ones
Battle-tested code that has been publicly deployed for years has had its bugs discovered and fixed through real-world usage
Institutional knowledge about past bugs is fragile — team members who fixed old bugs may no longer remember them or even be on the team
The desire to rewrite from scratch is a hallmark of inexperienced developers
money_quotes: Gartner seems to suffer the common but moronic fallacy that new or "completely rewritten" code is somehow less buggy than old code.
Completely rewriting it would just introduce another set of bugs that would take another few years to find.
IIS has been publically tested, for about six years now, on millions of web servers and with thousands of hackers trying to find bugs.
Chances are that nobody on the current IIS team even remembers the bugs they fixed five years ago.
Completely rewriting code is a big-time mistake common of immature developers with no real software experience.
key_insight: Rewriting battle-tested software from scratch doesn't eliminate bugs — it merely replaces known, fixed bugs with a fresh crop of unknown ones.
